text: The surgeon begins the procedure by separating the stomach from the left lobe of the liver. The right crus of the diaphragm is identified, and the sleeve is approached posteriorly.
The surgeon separates the sleeve from the surrounding inflammatory tissue near the angle of His, adjacent to the left hemidiaphragm.
The surgeon approaches the area of the fistula superiorly, observing the inflamed left hemidiaphragm above. The inflamed omentum is visible, and the fistula is identified with the suction tip.
The surgeon observes the opening at the sleeve esophageal junction, highlighting the area of concern related to the chronic fistula.
The surgeon begins transection by using a linear stapler to take the left gastric vessels. The hiatus is visibly freed, and the esophagus is dissected from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection posterior to the sleeve at the gastroesophageal junction, progressing towards the left side near the inflamed left hemidiaphragm and the fistula.
The surgeon rotates the sleeve medially and completely transects the fistula from the left hemidiaphragm, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues dissection until the left crus of the diaphragm is clearly identified. The hiatus on the left side is opened, revealing the anatomical structures.

The surgeon frees and mobilizes the esophagus intra-abdominally, preparing for the esophago-jejunal anastomosis. Both the right and left cruci are visible as the esophagus is moved into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the sleeve in its mid portion, visually separating it from surrounding tissues.
An opening is created in the proximal sleeve near the gastroesophageal junction, facilitating the passage of the anvil into the esophagus.
The anvil is inserted into the distal esophagus, preparing for the transection of the esophagus.
The surgeon transects the distal portion of the esophagus, carefully avoiding the anvil positioned within the esophagus.
The surgeon removes the specimen from the surgical field and places it in a container for pathological examination.
The anvil is delivered through the distal esophagus, and the surgeon prepares the small bowel for the esophago-jejunostomy anastomosis using a circular stapler.
The surgeon connects the anvil and circular stapler at the distal esophagus, closing them to complete the esophago-giginostomy anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the esophago-gastrostomy anastomosis using a circular stapler and then removes the stapler from the surgical site.
The surgeon reinforces the staple line adjacent to the esophago-jejunal anastomosis with interrupted sutures, ensuring secure closure of the small bowel.
Following the completion of the esophago-jejunostomy anastomosis, an endoscope is inserted to visually assess the integrity of the anastomosis and confirm the anatomical configuration.
The surgeon clamps the anastomosis and insufflates it with air underwater, observing no leakage of bubbles, indicating the integrity of the anastomosis.
